summary: Julia Evans reflects on her three-month experience at Hacker School (now Recurse Center), describing it as one of the best experiences of her life. She highlights the welcoming, positive atmosphere where nobody is told they can't do something. The environment's energy was so strong that her worry about lacking motivation proved laughably wrong. She credits the carefully chosen residents, skilled facilitators, and curated admissions process for creating a community of curious, friendly people. The piece serves as both a personal reflection and an enthusiastic recommendation for others to apply.
key_points: Hacker School's culture of 'yes, and!' meant nobody was told they couldn't do something because they didn't know enough, creating a fearless learning environment
The facilitators excelled not through domain expertise alone but through relentless curiosity, egolessness, and the ability to diagnose why someone doesn't understand something
Pairing was a powerful learning mechanism because it forced focus and enabled accidental learning from watching how others think and code
The admissions process was critical to the experience—curating for curiosity and friendliness created the community that made everything else work
The environment generated so much energy and ideas that the challenge was saying no, not finding motivation
money_quotes: The attitude was 'Oh, is something not working? How delightful! A learning opportunity!'.
I don't remember anyone ever being told that they couldn't do something because they didn't know enough.
I did more interesting projects in the 3 months of Hacker School than I did in the rest of my life combined.
They're very quick to admit if they're wrong and care about understanding, not about being right.
They have tons of practice at understanding why you don't understand.
She'd ask me super helpful leading questions and we'd fix my bugs! They are magicians.
Always keep learning, keep being curious, keep building.
key_insight: The best learning environments are built not from curriculum or credentials but from a deliberately curated culture of curiosity, positivity, and mutual respect.
